problem_statement: LLM-based code reasoning agents produce incorrect or hallucinated answers when explaining code, judging equivalence, or validating auto-generated code, so their outputs must be manually verified before they can be trusted. The paper addresses automatically validating an agent's reasoning steps to reduce this manual oversight burden.
magnitude_evidence: "the agent hallucinates on 40% of the problems (8/20)" for program equivalence queries; for MSAN bugs 12/100 runs had invalid explanations by human judgment
insufficiency_evidence: LLM-as-judge "is not foolproof either and is still susceptible to the LLM judge hallucinating"; and manual review is costly — answers "need to be manually verified before they can be trusted" requiring "human effort" and "slower developer productivity"; the formal verifier itself has "perfect precision and low recall" (validated only 18/88, later 36/88)
solution_mechanisms: extract formal representation of agent's reasoning
verify reasoning steps with formal verification tools
post-facto verification of LLM prose reasoning
iterative formalization to synthesize missing predicates
notes: Text contains minor PDF-extraction garbling (spaced-out code, figure captions interleaved) but is readable; one sentence appears truncated ("falsely concludes the programs").
